content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 8 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 205–206 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_008_page_001.jpg, DEU_008_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 149

---

1 Every commandment which I am commanding you today you shall be careful to do, in order that you may live and multiply and go in and possess the land which the LORD swore to your fathers.

2 And you shall remember all the way which the LORD your God led you these forty years in the wilderness, in order to humble you, to test you, to know what was in your heart — whether you would keep his commandments or not.

3 And he humbled you and let you be hungry and fed you the manna, which you did not know nor did your fathers know, in order to make you know that not by bread alone does a man live [lo al ha-lechem levaddo yichyeh ha-adam] — but by every going-forth of the mouth of the LORD a man lives.

4 Your garment did not wear out upon you and your foot did not swell these forty years.

5 And you shall know in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD your God disciplines you.

6 And you shall keep the commandments of the LORD your God to walk in his ways and to fear him.

7 For the LORD your God is bringing you into a good land [eretz tovah] — a land of wadis of water, of springs and depths going out in the valley and in the hill-country;

8 a land of wheat and barley and vines and fig-trees and pomegranates, a land of olive-oil and honey;

9 a land in which you will eat bread without scarceness, you will lack nothing in it — a land whose stones are iron and from whose hills you may hew copper.

10 And you shall eat and be satisfied and bless the LORD your God for the good land which he gave to you.

11 Be careful lest you forget the LORD your God by not keeping his commandments and his ordinances and his statutes which I am commanding you today,

12 lest you eat and be satisfied and build good houses and settle in them,

13 and your cattle and your flocks multiply, and silver and gold multiply for you, and all that you have multiplies —

14 and your heart grows high and you forget the LORD your God who brought you out from the land of Egypt, from the house of slavery,

15 who led you through the great and fearful wilderness — [the land of] the fiery serpent and the scorpion and the thirsty ground where there was no water — who brought water out for you from the flint rock,

16 who fed you in the wilderness with manna which your fathers did not know, in order to humble you and in order to test you, to do you good in your end.

17 And you say in your heart, "My power and the might of my hand has made for me this wealth!"

18 But you shall remember the LORD your God, for it is he who gives you power to make wealth, in order to establish his covenant which he swore to your fathers, as [it is] this day.

19 And it shall be, if you indeed forget the LORD your God and walk after other gods and serve them and bow down to them — I testify against you today that you shall surely perish.

20 Like the nations which the LORD is destroying from before you, so you shall perish — because you did not hearken to the voice of the LORD your God.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.3 — lo al ha-lechem levaddo:** Hebrew לֹא עַל-הַלֶּחֶם לְבַדּוֹ יִחְיֶה הָאָדָם — "not by bread alone does a man live." The word order in Hebrew places the negation first with emphatic force: "not by bread alone." The LXX renders οὐκ ἐπ᾽ ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος (Vat. p.149) — this is the exact phrase cited by Yeshua/Jesus in the gospel temptation narratives. "Every going-forth of the mouth" (כָּל-מוֹצָא פִּי יְהוָה) is a rigidly literal rendering; other translations smooth this to "everything that proceeds from the mouth of the LORD" but the construct "going-forth" (motsa') is preserved here.

**V.7 — eretz tovah:** Hebrew אֶרֶץ טוֹבָה — "a good land." The description in vv.7–9 is a seven-element catalogue of abundance. The seven items (wheat, barley, vine, fig, pomegranate, olive-oil, honey) correspond to the traditional "seven species" (shivat ha-minim) of the land. The LXX (Vat. p.149) preserves the same seven-element list.

**V.9 — "bread without scarceness":** Hebrew לֹא-בְמִסְכֵּנֻת — lit. "not in scarceness/poverty." The rare word מִסְכֵּנֻת (miskenuth) means scarcity, privation. Rendered literally. The LXX renders μετὰ πτωχείας οὐ φάγῃ (you will not eat in poverty).

**V.9 — iron and copper:** The text reads אֶרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אֲבָנֶיהָ בַרְזֶל — "a land whose stones are iron" — and וּמֵהֲרָרֶיהָ תַּחְצֹב נְחֹשֶׁת — "from whose hills you will hew copper/bronze." The Hebrew נְחֹשֶׁת (nechosheth) can mean either copper or bronze; "copper" is the more literal mining-context reading. The LXX renders χαλκόν (bronze/copper).

**V.15 — "fiery serpent":** Hebrew נָחָשׁ שָׂרָף — "serpent of fire" or "fiery serpent." The שָׂרָף (saraph) here is the same root as the seraphim of Isa 6. The LXX renders ὄφιν δάκνοντα (biting serpent) — a paraphrase that loses the "fiery" dimension. MT preserved.

**V.17 — "My power and the might of my hand":** Hebrew כֹּחִי וְעֹצֶם יָדִי — the pair of nouns koach and otsem create a rhetorically strong merism of human self-sufficiency. The LXX renders ἡ ἰσχύς μου καὶ τὸ κράτος τῆς χειρός μου (my strength and the might of my hand). MT and LXX agree closely.

**V.18 — "power to make wealth":** Hebrew כֹּחַ לַעֲשׂוֹת חָיִל — lit. "power to make/do valor/wealth." The word חַיִל (chayil) has a wide semantic range (strength, valor, wealth, army). In this economic context "wealth" is primary; the ambiguity is noted.

**Source note:** The Vaticanus image for this chapter (Vat.gr.1209 p.149) is the same physical page as the tail of chapter 7; chapter 8 begins partway through that page. The LC material for this chapter appears on pages 205–206, with the chapter opening visible in the LC p.205 image (DEU_008_page_001.jpg) and continuing to p.206 (DEU_008_page_002.jpg).

— EMET
